records.ety: [OE. fecchen, AS. feccan, perh. the same word as fetian; or cf. facian to wish to get, OFries. faka to prepare. √77. Cf. Fet, v. t.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Fetched 2; p. pr. & vb. n.. Fetching.]

defs: To bear toward the person speaking, or the person or thing from whose point of view the action is contemplated; to go and bring; to get.
quotes: Time will run back and fetch the age of gold.

He called to her, and said, Fetch me, I pray thee, a little water in a vessel, that I may drink. And as she was going to fetch it he called to her, and said, Bring me, I pray thee, a morsel of bread in thine hand.

defs: To obtain as price or equivalent; to sell for.
quotes: Our native horses were held in small esteem, and fetched low prices.

defs: To recall from a swoon; to revive; -- sometimes with to; as, to fetch a man to.
quotes: Fetching men again when they swoon.
The sudden trip in wrestling that fetches a man to the ground.

defs: To bring to accomplishment; to achieve; to make; to perform, with certain objects; as, to fetch a compass; to fetch a leap; to fetch a sigh.
quotes: I'll fetch a turn about the garden.
He fetches his blow quick and sure.

defs: To bring or get within reach by going; to reach; to arrive at; to attain; to reach by sailing.
quotes: Meantine flew our ships, and straight we fetched The siren's isle.

defs: To cause to come; to bring to a particular state.
quotes: They could n't fetch the butter in the churn.

defs: To bring one's self; to make headway; to veer; as, to fetch about; to fetch to windward.
A stratagem by which a thing is indirectly brought to pass, or by which one thing seems intended and another is done; a trick; an artifice.
quotes: Every little fetch of wit and criticism.

defs: The apparation of a living person; a wraith.
quotes: The very fetch and ghost of Mrs. Gamp.

defs: The unobstructed region of the ocean over which the wind blows to generate waves.
The length of such a region.
